channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Phones in Hand, India’s Young Protesters Blast Through Modi’s Media Control
item.description: Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s tightly constructed image is being punctured as a Gen Z-led protest movement uses videos and memes to seize the digital narrative.
item.dc:creator: Mujib Mashal
item.media:credit: Saumya Khandelwal for The New York Times
item.media:description: Recording a video with a masked protester in New Delhi on Thursday.

item.title: In Kyrgyzstan, Girls Compete at Kok-Boru, a Game Like Polo
item.description: Here’s what happened when some teenage girls dared to compete in the dangerous sport of “kok-boru.”
item.dc:creator: Alexander M. Thompson and Sevindj Nurkiyazova
item.media:description: From left, Begimai Bakirova and her teammates Aisuluu Birimkulova, 17, and Shoola Kenenbaeva, 16, laughing after a game.

item.title: ‘We’re Trapped in Hell’: Iranians Say Life Is Put On Hold by Months of War
item.description: Devastated by cycles of violence and economic crisis, many are giving up on work and their futures as the United States batters Iran again.
item.dc:creator: Leily Nikounazar and Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Street vendors beneath a billboard of the former supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, in Tehran last week.

item.title: Venezuela’s Earthquakes Show Wealth Divide, Even in Death
item.description: Latin America’s deep-seated inequalities prevailed over the grimly equalizing force of the Venezuela earthquakes, and set even the dead apart.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michell Mendoza, 16, right, standing next to a truck holding her mother’s coffin after being turned away from a cemetery.

item.title: 5 Killed in West Bank Shooting, Officials Say, Among the Deadliest in Months
item.description: The authorities said four Palestinians and an Israeli were killed in the incident. The details of events remain unclear, with officials offering differing accounts.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon
item.media:description: The body of a Palestinian man being brought to a hospital in the West Bank city of Nablus on Friday. Five people were killed in a shooting in nearby Tell on Friday.

item.title: As Canada Opens Gordie Howe International Bridge, Americans Are Told to Stay Home
item.description: After a delay ordered by President Trump and new tariffs against Canada, the bridge between Windsor, Ontario, and Detroit opens in “underwhelming” fashion on Friday.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.category: Gordie Howe International Bridge (Detroit, Mich and Windsor, Ontario)
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Gordie Howe International Bridge under construction last year in Windsor, Ontario.

item.title: Faced With Heat and Wildfires, France Turns to Spain to Learn How to Cope
item.description: A French government delegation will travel to its southern neighbor to learn how it copes with long spells of hot weather.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trying to stay cool in Paris during a heat wave last month.

item.title: South Korea A.I. Billionaire Ordered to Pay More Than Half a Billion Dollars in ‘Divorce of the Century’
item.description: A South Korean court awarded $644 million to Roh Soh-yeong, who had sought much more from her ex-husband, Chey Tae-won, the head of the conglomerate behind SK Hynix.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Yonhap, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: SK Group Chairman Chey Tae-won, seen arriving at a court in Seoul in June, is estimated to have a fortune worth more than $5 billion.

item.title: Modi Caught Off Guard as India’s Economic Failings Inflame Young People
item.description: Prime Minister Narendra Modi was busy trying to put out other fires when a student movement suddenly exploded. There are no easy solutions to the problems behind it.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.media:credit: Saumya Khandelwal for The New York Times
item.media:description: People walk past protest banners in New Delhi on Thursday.

item.title: Oil-Rich Saudi Arabia Isn’t Short on Energy. Here’s Why It Wants to Go Nuclear.
item.description: Opponents warn that a U.S.-Saudi deal could be a step toward the kingdom building an atomic bomb. But there are other reasons for the world’s largest oil exporter to diversify its energy mix.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Overlooking Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, in 2024.

item.title: Why I Sought Out a Taliban Commander I Had Fought
item.description: First as a Marine and then as a journalist, I spent years tied to Afghanistan. I returned one last time to speak to a former enemy in search of answers, and perhaps closure.
item.dc:creator: Thomas Gibbons-Neff
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jim Huylebroek for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mullah Abdul Rahim Gulab in Marja, Afghanistan, in 2021.

item.title: A New Target for Ukraine’s Long-Range Attacks: Russia’s Version of Amazon
item.description: Kyiv is escalating its air war by striking commercial warehouses, trying to bring the conflict closer to home for ordinary Russians.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins and Alina Lobzina
item.media:description: Smoke rising from a fire at a Wildberries logistics complex in Elektrostal, near Moscow, on July 18, after a Ukrainian drone attack.

item.title: For South Korea, U.S.-Saudi Nuclear Deal Exposes a Double Standard
item.description: President Trump has put Saudi Arabia on the path to enriching uranium on its own soil, a demand South Korea has been making for years.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: A beach near Wolseong Nuclear Power Plant in Gyeongju, South Korea, in 2022. The country has one of the biggest nuclear power industries in the world.

item.title: Bangladesh’s President Resigns Amid Uncertainty Over Ex-Leader’s Plans
item.description: President Mohammed Shahabuddin stepped down as speculation swirled over the possible return of his onetime ally Sheikh Hasina, who was ousted as prime minister and fled to India.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim and Saif Hasnat
item.media:description: President Mohammed Shahabuddin of Bangladesh in Dhaka in February.

item.title: Russia and Ukraine Trade Blows With Missiles and Drones
item.description: Ukrainian strikes set fire to warehouses and other sites in Russia, and Russian bombing rocked Ukraine’s capital, as the two countries escalated a deadly air campaign.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora

item.title: With Hopes High for New H.I.V. Prevention Pill, Merck Takes Steps to Ensure Access
item.description: Companies in Africa and India will make generic versions of the pill that could cost as little as $5 per person per year. But access to the drug and other new products in much of Latin America remains uncertain.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers speaking with students about H.I.V. prevention drugs at the University of Zambia in Lusaka in March.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Qasim Nauman, Farnaz Fassihi and Erika Solomon

item.title: On Instagram, Travelers Are Taking Risky Cross-Continental Journeys. What Could Go Wrong?
item.description: Travel accounts that feature risky, cross-continental journeys are proliferating on social media. Security experts have serious concerns.
item.dc:creator: Ryley Ober

item.title: Prominent Indian Activist Breaks Hunger Strike After 26 Days
item.description: The activist, Sonam Wangchuk, said that he had broken his fast “after long negotiations” with officials and “in view of possible violence in the country.”
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:description: The activist Sonam Wangchuk started striking in solidarity with a wider protest that began after leaked test questions led India in May to cancel its medical school entrance exams.

item.title: Iran Rejects U.S. Cease-Fire Deal Presented by Iraqi Leader
item.description: The details of the proposal were not immediately clear, but Iranian officials said that it was the only offer on the table.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi and Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi of Iraq meeting with President Trump at the White House this month.

item.title: Senators Ask Hegseth to Fully Account for U.S. Troops Killed or Wounded in Iran
item.description: The 12 Democratic senators on the Armed Services Committee said Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s department had not provided “timely and comprehensive” reporting on casualties during the war.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth at Dover Air Force Base on Wednesday at a dignified transfer for U.S. Army soldiers killed in action during the war in Iran.

item.title: Trump, His Money, and the World
item.description: Donald Trump’s growing wealth while in office could have an impact on anti-corruption efforts around the world.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: What to Know About Iran and the Houthis
item.description: The Yemeni militant group appeared to open a new front in the conflict between its backer, Iran, and the United States, by attacking ships and announcing a blockade on a strategic waterway.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Yemeni supporters of the Iran-backed Houthi group during a protest last week in Sanaa, Yemen.

item.title: The Defining Image of India’s Gen Z Protests
item.description: A new protest movement is spreading across India, and the video of a woman blocking a police van has captured the resolve of the young people leading it.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:description: Rhiya Ahir blocking a police van carrying detained protesters in Mumbai, India, on Wednesday.

item.title: Strikes Against Saudi Tankers in the Red Sea Send Oil Past $100 a Barrel
item.description: President Trump threatened attacks on Houthi militants after the Iranian ally said it had hit two Saudi Arabian-flagged vessels.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Leo Sands and Thomas Fuller
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Saudi authorities on Thursday confirmed a strike on one of its ships, the Encelia.

item.title: Trump’s New Demand for Nuclear Deal Puts Saudis in a Bind
item.description: Saudi Arabia has long said that a condition of normalizing relations with Israel would be a pathway toward Palestinian statehood, a prospect that Israel’s current government has rejected.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: President Trump welcoming Mohammed bin Salman, the crown prince of Saudi Arabia, to the White House during a 2025 state visit.

item.title: The Tate Brothers Are Losing Right-Wing Allies After Their Arrest
item.description: After Andrew and Tristan Tate, prominent leaders of the so-called manosphere, were arrested in Miami, many of their onetime allies on the American right pulled back support.
item.dc:creator: Megan Twohey, Isabella Kwai, Matei Barbulescu and Alan Feuer
item.media:description: Andrew Tate, right, and Tristan Tate in Bucharest, Romania, in 2025.

item.title: Chaos Breaks Out at Border Crossings With Iraq
item.description: Videos verified by The New York Times show border crossings in southern Iraq becoming the latest target in the hostilities in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: James McManagan and Sanjana Varghese
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Zelensky’s Dizzying U-Turn Is the Latest Zigzag in a Presidency Full of Them
item.description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine has pivoted from unpopular moves before, but a recent decision about military leadership and strategy could weaken his position.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine during a visit to Paris this month. His country is in the grips of some of the worst turbulence in domestic politics since Russia’s all-out invasion in 2022.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Shirin Hakim and Farnaz Fassihi

item.title: U.S.-Saudi Deal Prompts Fears of a Global Nuclear Arms Race
item.description: In Europe, Asia and the Middle East, countries seek nuclear capabilities to hedge against aggressive neighbors and a receding Washington, potentially creating a more freewheeling era.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: A French nuclear submarine. France has expanded its nuclear deterrence to cover some allies as the United States has pulled back on European security.

item.title: A Russian Spy, Suddenly Cast Into the Spotlight, Flees Japan
item.description: The military intelligence officer left the country after The Times reported that he was using the Aeroflot airline office in Tokyo to conduct espionage.
item.dc:creator: Michael Schwirtz, Adam Goldman and Jane Bradley
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Toranomon Kotohira Tower is home to the Aeroflot airline office in Tokyo.

item.title: Should Travelers Give Up on Summer in Western Europe Because of the Heat?
item.description: A favorite vacation option may be at risk because of rising temperatures. Even those sticking with their summer plans agree: More air conditioning would help.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Seeking shade in the Place des Vosges in Paris during the June heat wave.

item.title: ‘We Will All Die From Ebola Here’: Virus Stalks Crowded Camps in Congo
item.description: There are a million people displaced by violence in the epicenter. They live in dangerous conditions and are fighting the virus with very little clean water.
item.dc:creator: Finbarr O’Reilly
item.media:credit: Finbarr O'Reilly for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. Military Presence in Jordan Makes It Iranian Target
item.description: Relying heavily on U.S. aid and protection, Jordan is unlikely to restrict the American military presence there, despite escalating Iranian airstrikes.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: American and Jordanian soldiers participating in a military exercise northeast of Amman in 2022.

item.title: Japan’s Leader Invokes Her Hero, Shinzo Abe, to Drive Nationalist Agenda
item.description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is leveraging her ties to Mr. Abe, the slain Japanese leader, as she pushes to reshape defense and the economy.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández
item.media:credit: Kentaro Takahashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sanae Takaichi has frequently leveraged her ties to Shinzo Abe.

item.title: Debate Over Ancient Iranian Jewelry Worn by Zendaya to ‘Odyssey’ Event
item.description: The actress wore earrings to an event for “The Odyssey” that a London gallery has said were made from an ancient artifact.
item.dc:creator: Yola Mzizi
item.media:credit: Scott A Garfitt/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Zendaya at an event for “The Odyssey” in London this month.

item.title: Houthis Claim Strikes on 2 Saudi Oil Tankers in Red Sea
item.description: The Saudi authorities confirmed a strike on one of the ships. These are the first claimed attacks since the Iranian-backed Yemeni group announced a blockade on Saudi Arabia.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A satellite image of the Bab al-Mandab Strait earlier this month.

item.title: What Visuals Show About Recent Damage to U.S. Military Sites in the Middle East
item.description: Video footage and satellite images reveal new details about Iranian strikes on U.S. targets during the latest flare-up in fighting.
item.dc:creator: Aric Toler and Christoph Koettl
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Three Airports Plan to Ditch T.S.A. Agents Amid Push for Private Security
item.description: The airports have opted in to the agency’s new security model, an agency spokesperson confirmed on Wednesday. A union official said the move could jeopardize public safety.
item.dc:creator: Ryley Ober
item.media:description: Tampa International is one of three U.S. airports opting in to a new program.

item.title: Saudi Arabia’s Nuclear Plans
item.description: The U.S. will help the Saudis develop a civilian nuclear program, with uncertain consequences for the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Dubai Firm Cuts Deal for New Port to Bypass Strait of Hormuz
item.description: A Dubai-based logistics giant unveiled a deal on Wednesday to develop shipping terminals on the United Arab Emirates’ east coast.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tomas Munita for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ships in the Gulf of Oman east of the United Arab Emirates this week.

item.title: U.S. Troops, Seriously Wounded in Recent Strikes, Were Flown to Germany for Help
item.description: The seriously wounded service members are among the roughly 100 American troops that the Defense Department recently said were injured by Iranian attacks since early July.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Donald Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and other U.S. officials attending the arrival of U.S. Army soldiers killed in recent Iranian strikes.

item.title: Hitler’s Birthplace in Austria Is Now a Police Station
item.description: The government repurposed the 17th-century building in hopes of preventing the site from becoming an attraction for Nazi sympathizers.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses

item.title: Israeli Banks Threaten to Cut Off Palestinian Economy
item.description: Two Israeli financial firms have long served as partners to Palestinian banks, handling transactions that make possible the West Bank’s imports and exports.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Adam Rasgon
item.media:credit: Simon Beni/Middle East Images, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Bank Hapoalim tower in Tel Aviv. Bank Hapoalim and Israel Discount Bank have written to their Palestinian counterparts saying they will end banking relationships with them.

item.title: Why Are Young People in India Protesting?
item.description: A youth-led movement angry over the education system and a lack of jobs is posing a rare challenge to a government that has ruled for more than a decade with a firm hand.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:description: Activists and supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party clashed with police officers during a protest in New Delhi on Monday.

item.title: How the Trump Administration’s Nuclear Deal With Saudi Arabia May Work
item.description: The Trump administration announced an agreement with Saudi Arabia on a civilian nuclear program. Such deals, known as 123 Agreements, are designed to prevent the development of weapons.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: President Trump with the Saudi crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman, in Washington last year.

item.title: Canadian Wildfire Evacuees Outraged Over Lacking Support and Communication
item.description: Residents of northwestern Ontario say their warnings about fires they spotted were dismissed and official information as the flames spread often didn’t come at all.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Cole Burston
item.media:credit: Cole Burston for The New York Times
item.media:description: Swaths of burned forest from the Dryden 35 Wildfire, part of a series of wildfires that forced many to flee their homes in Northwestern Ontario this month.

item.title: Rubio and Chinese Diplomat Make Plans for Trump and Xi’s Washington Summit
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio spent much of a meeting with China’s foreign minister discussing the summit, but did not bring up President Trump’s claims of election interference by China.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:description: President Donald Trump with Xi Jinping in Beijing in May.

item.title: Daniel Siad, Model Scout Who Connected Women to Epstein, Is Found Dead in France
item.description: Daniel Siad, who was found dead on Monday, repeatedly introduced Jeffrey Epstein to young women, according to messages he exchanged with Mr. Epstein. Mr. Siad had denied wrongdoing.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: A screenshot from a messaging app with a photo of Daniel Siad. The screenshot was included in the files released by the U.S. Department of Justice on Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Coveted U.S. Nuclear Deal Is a Coup for Saudi Crown Prince
item.description: Saudi Arabia has for years sought a deal to obtain American nuclear technology. The agreement, signed on Wednesday, could deepen U.S.-Saudi ties for decades to come.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prince Mohammed bin Salman, the de facto Saudi ruler, and President Trump at the White House in November.

item.title: Trump’s 50% Tariffs on Canada: What to Know, and What’s Next
item.description: The president said punishing 50 percent tariffs would begin by Aug. 19, a significant escalation of the trade war between Canada and the U.S. that he started over a year ago.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: President Trump and Mark Carney in the Oval Office last year.

item.title: U.S.-Saudi Nuclear Deal Stokes Israeli Concerns of Mideast Arms Race
item.description: An Israeli minister sought to downplay the agreement, while critics said it could spur other regional countries to pursue a nuclear program, and potentially the creation of a bomb.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer and Aaron Boxerman
item.media:description: Nir Barkat, Israel’s minister of economy, in Tel Aviv last year. He has been the most senior Israeli government official to comment so far on the expected U.S.-Saudi nuclear deal.
